topic: How accurate labour cost data changed the way one owner made pricing decisions
format: Open with a data point about Oman businesses and explain the root cause behind it
post:
A business owner in Muscat thought he was running a 22% margin. His real number was 6%.

He'd been quoting projects the same way for three years — materials cost, rough time estimate, add a margin. It felt like pricing. It wasn't.

When he finally connected his WPS payroll data with actual hours worked per job, the number was uncomfortable. His margin on mid-size contracts wasn't 22%. It was 6%.

He wasn't growing. He was subsidising his clients.

The problem wasn't carelessness. His data was spread across spreadsheets, WhatsApp threads, and payroll files that never talked to each other. Nobody sees the real picture when the numbers live in five different places.

Once payroll, workforce hours, and project costs were in one system — HR software built for Oman's WPS requirements — he repriced within a week. Three months later, margin was back above 19%. Same team. Same clients. Just accurate numbers.

The software didn't fix his business. Knowing the truth did.

If you're still estimating labour costs from memory or last month's payroll summary, you're pricing blind. And the gap between what you think you're making and what you're actually making tends to be a lot wider than people expect.

What would change if you knew your real cost per employee, per project, per month?
